title: Integrate by separation

confidence.label: Grounded
confidence.meaning: symbolically proven to follow from the previous step

confidence.label: Domain
confidence.meaning: valid by domain knowledge, not a symbolic identity the CAS can check
confidence.reason: valid by domain knowledge — the CAS could not check this symbolically; a domain expert accepts it (confidence 100%): If two differential forms are equal, their integrals must also be equal.

steps.justification: constants pull out of the integral

confidence.label: Grounded
confidence.meaning: symbolically proven to follow from the previous step
confidence.reason: symbolically equivalent to the previous step

overall_confidence.label: Domain
overall_confidence.meaning: valid by domain knowledge, not a symbolic identity the CAS can check

__power_3.name: \frac{1}{v}
__power_3.description: This term represents the reciprocal of the variable $v$ that is being integrated.

v.name: v
v.description: This is one of the dependent variables being separated for integration.

dv.name: dv
dv.description: This is the differential of the variable $v$, indicating integration with respect to $v$.

dh.name: dh
dh.description: This is the differential of the variable $h$, indicating integration with respect to $h$.

k.name: k
k.description: This is a constant coefficient in the expression being integrated with respect to $h$.

__power_6.name: e^{\frac{\left(-1\right) h}{H}}
__power_6.description: This is an exponential term involving the variable $h$ and the constant $H$.

e.name: e
e.description: This represents Euler's number, the base of the natural logarithm.

h.name: h
h.description: This is the other dependent variable being separated for integration.

__power_9.name: \frac{1}{H}
__power_9.description: This term represents the reciprocal of the constant $H$ within the exponent.

H.name: H
H.description: This is a constant parameter found in the exponent of the exponential term.

__integral_2.name: \int \frac{1}{v}\, dv
__integral_2.description: This represents the integral of $\frac{1}{v}$ with respect to $v$.

__integral_4.name: \int \left(- e^{\frac{\left(-1\right) h}{H}} k\right)\, dh
__integral_4.description: This represents the integral of the expression $\left(- e^{\frac{\left(-1\right) h}{H}} k\right)$ with respect to $h$

goal: This derivation shows how to prepare a separated differential equation for integration by applying the integral operator to both sides and factoring out constants.
followups: Evaluate the integrals on both sides of the equation.
Explain why we can factor out the constant $k$.
What would be the next step if we had initial conditions?
How would this process change for a different type of differential equation?
